framework.jurisdiction: Global / international (OECD Adherents and non-Adherents invited to take account)
framework.name: Recommendation of the Council on Artificial Intelligence
framework.issuing_body: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Council
framework.scope: This OECD Recommendation sets out principles for trustworthy AI and policy guidance for governments and AI actors. It applies across sectors and AI system lifecycles, focusing on responsible stewardship, human rights, transparency, robustness, accountability, and international interoperability rather than sector-specific mandates.
framework.applies_to: OECD member countries, adhering partner economies, and non-member jurisdictions invited to take account of the Recommendation; also used as a reference by AI developers, deployers, suppliers, users, and policymakers across sectors and organization sizes.
framework.penalties: No legal penalties or fines are specified because the instrument is non-binding. OECD Council Recommendations operate as political commitments with peer-pressure, monitoring, and reporting; the 2024 revision notes implementation review by the OECD Digital Policy Committee, but no maximum penalty applies.

control.statement: AI stakeholders should proactively engage in responsible stewardship of trustworthy AI to pursue beneficial outcomes for people and the planet, including inclusive growth, well-being, sustainable development, and environmental sustainability.
AI actors should respect the rule of law, human rights, democratic and human-centred values throughout the AI system lifecycle and implement mechanisms and safeguards for human agency and oversight.

AI actors should commit to transparency and responsible disclosure, providing meaningful information about AI system capabilities, limitations, data sources, logic, and avenues to challenge outputs where appropriate.
AI systems should be robust, secure, and safe throughout their lifecycle, with mechanisms to override, repair, or decommission systems safely if they risk causing undue harm or undesired behaviour.
AI actors should be accountable for the proper functioning of AI systems and compliance with the principles, including traceability of datasets, processes, and decisions across the lifecycle.

Governments should consider long-term public investment and encourage private investment in AI R&D, open science, open-source tools, and representative open datasets that respect privacy and data protection.
Governments should promote an agile, interoperable governance and policy environment for AI, including experimentation, outcome-based approaches, and cross-jurisdictional cooperation.
Governments should build human capacity and support fair labour transition measures, including skills development, social dialogue, and worker support as AI is deployed.
Governments should cooperate internationally to advance the principles, share AI knowledge, develop global technical standards, and use comparable indicators to measure AI development and deployment.
